perturbation.original_text: Agent shall maintain, at Agent's cost (as an Expense) and in such amounts as Agent currently has in effect, commercial general liability policies covering injuries to persons and property in or in connection with Agent's agency at the Stores and shall cause Merchant to be named as an additional insured with respect to such policies. Agent shall deliver to Merchant certificates evidencing such insurance policies setting forth the duration thereof and naming Merchant as an additional insured, in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to Merchant.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent shall endeavor to maintain commercial general liability policies covering injuries to persons and property in or in connection with Agent's agency at the Stores and may cause Merchant to be named as an additional insured with respect to such policies. Agent will use its best efforts to deliver to Merchant certificates evidencing such insurance policies setting forth the duration thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Agent shall maintain' to 'Agent shall endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'shall cause Merchant to be named' with 'may cause Merchant to be named' makes the additional insured status optional. Removing the requirement for the certificate to be 'in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to Merchant' further reduces Agent's responsibility. These changes create ambiguity about the level of insurance coverage and protection Merchant receives.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Fiduciary Duty and Negligence
perturbation.law_explanation: As the contract is under Delaware law, it is implied that the Agent has a duty of care that may be deemed to be breached should the Agent only 'endeavor' to maintain insurance, and the Merchant is not automatically named as an additional insured or does not have the power of consenting to the form of the certificate. The Agent's discretion and weaker obligations may conflict with the expected standard of care and create non-compliance with corporate duties under Delaware law, specifically where failing to secure adequate insurance puts the Merchant at risk. The business judgment rule may offer some protection, but if negligence or lack of good faith is proven, it fails.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall maintain' to 'shall endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation to maintain insurance. Replacing 'shall cause Merchant to be named' with 'may cause Merchant to be named' makes the additional insured status optional, and removing the requirement for the certificate to be 'in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to Merchant' reduces Agent's responsibility. The Law Explanation asserts that, under Delaware law, this weakens the Agent's duty of care and puts the Merchant at risk. Snippet 1 describes the powers of the board of directors, and the need for them to manage the corporation; the changes made to the contract could be argued to negatively impact the Board's ability to manage risk because the agent is not as strongly obligated to ensure insurance coverage. The agent may no longer be acting in good faith. Thus, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Following, and subject to, the entry of the Approval Order, Agent may advertise the Sale as a "store closing", "sale on everything", "everything must go" or similar-themed sale, as dictated by the Approval Order.
perturbation.changed_text: Following, and subject to, the entry of the Approval Order, Agent may advertise the Sale with themes that are generally suggestive of a clearance event.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for specific phrases that are commonly understood to mean a liquidation sale. The modified text replaces these specific phrases with 'themes that are generally suggestive of a clearance event,' which is vague and could allow for advertising that does not clearly indicate the store is closing. This creates ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: Advertising a 'clearance event' when the store is actually going out of business could be seen as a deceptive practice, violating Section 5 of the FTC Act (15 U.S.C. § 45), which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. The ambiguity created by the modified text could mislead consumers into believing they are getting a better deal than they actually are, or that the sale is temporary, when it is in fact a permanent store closure.

Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces specific phrases indicating a 'store closing' with the vague term 'themes that are generally suggestive of a clearance event.' This creates ambiguity, potentially misleading consumers. The LLM explanation correctly points out that advertising a 'clearance event' when the store is going out of business could violate Section 5 of the FTC Act (15 U.S.C. § 45), which prohibits deceptive practices. The scraped law snippet 1 confirms the FTC's power to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce'. The change weakens the original text's clarity about the sale's nature, increasing the risk of misleading consumers, thus contradicting the FTC Act's aim to prevent deceptive practices. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguities Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Agent shall keep Store premises and surrounding areas clear and orderly consistent with present practices.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent shall endeavor to keep Store premises and surrounding areas reasonably clear.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a relatively clear standard: maintain cleanliness 'consistent with present practices'. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall keep' with 'shall endeavor to keep' (weakening the obligation) and 'clear and orderly consistent with present practices' with 'reasonably clear' (a vague and subjective standard).
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by making the cleanliness standard subjective and weakening the obligation to maintain it, could lead to non-compliance with OSHA regulation 29 CFR § 1910.22, which requires workplaces to be kept clean, orderly, and in a sanitary condition. The 'reasonably clear' standard is open to interpretation and may not meet the level of cleanliness required by OSHA to ensure employee safety.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.22 - General requirements. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.22 - General requirements. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Subpart: 1910 Subpart D Subpart Title: Walking-Working Surfaces Standard Number: 1910.22 Title: General requirements. GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.22(a) Surface conditions . The employer must ensure: 1910.22(a)(1) All places of employment, passageways, storerooms, service rooms, and walking-working surfaces are kept in a clean, orderly, and sanitary condition. 1910.22(a)(2) The floor of each workroom is maintained in a clean and, to the extent feasible, in a dry condition. When wet processes are used, drainage must be maintained and, to the extent feasible, dry standing places, such as false floors, platforms, and mats must be provided. 1910.22(a)(3) Walking-working surfaces are maintained free of hazards such as sharp or protruding objects, loose boards, corrosion, leaks, spills, snow, and ice. 1910.22(b) Loads . The employer must ensure that each walking-working surface can support the maximum intended load for that surface. 1910.22(c) Access and egress . The employer must provide, and ensure each employee uses, a safe means of access and egress to and from walking-working surfaces. 1910.22(d) Inspection, maintenance, and repair . The employer must ensure: 1910.22(d)(1) Walking-working surfaces are inspected, regularly and as necessary, and maintained in a safe condition; 1910.22(d)(2) Hazardous conditions on walking-working surfaces are corrected or repaired before an employee uses the walking-working surface again. If the correction or repair cannot be made immediately, the hazard must be guarded to prevent employees from using the walking-working surface until the hazard is corrected or repaired; and 1910.22(d)(3) When any correction or repair involves the structural integrity of the walking-working surface, a qualified person performs or supe

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain a clean and orderly store, potentially leading to non-compliance with OSHA regulations. The original text states 'Agent shall keep Store premises...clear and orderly consistent with present practices,' establishing a clear obligation. The changed text, 'Agent shall endeavor to keep Store premises and surrounding areas reasonably clear,' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 1910.22(a)(1), states that 'All places of employment, passageways, storerooms, service rooms, and walking-working surfaces are kept in a clean, orderly, and sanitary condition.' The modified text's 'reasonably clear' standard is subjective and could fail to meet OSHA's requirement for a 'clean, orderly, and sanitary condition,' creating a contradiction. The change undermines the legally mandated standard.
